name: Work Shaping
tagline: Choose the smallest honest amount of process before coding.
focus: Process calibration before coding
best_used_when: You need to decide whether a task is tiny or substantial.
You need to choose between chat, a local note, or a GitHub issue.
You want to make architecture, review, or public-writing lanes explicit.
typical_prompts: Is this a tiny task or substantial work?
Should this go into GitHub or stay in chat?
Do we need a checklist, an execution brief, or a full architecture pack?

name: Idea Validation
tagline: Turn a raw product idea into a lean validation brief.
focus: Idea validation before PRD or implementation
best_used_when: The idea is interesting but still fuzzy.
You need a named buyer, pain, wedge, and proof path.
You want to test demand before turning the idea into a build order.
typical_prompts: Validate this idea before we build it.
Who is the buyer and what painful recurring job are we removing?
What is the smallest wedge and the fastest proof path?

name: Product Shaping
tagline: Use the smallest product framework that resolves the next decision.
focus: Product framing after basic validation
best_used_when: The idea has some signal, but strategy or positioning is still fuzzy.
You need to surface risky assumptions or choose experiments.
You want a concrete next lane instead of generic PM discussion.
typical_prompts: Shape the product side without overdoing process.
Which framework fits this decision best?
Map the risky assumptions and tell me the next lane.

name: Product Council
tagline: Stress-test a product decision with explicit multi-lens councils.
focus: High-impact product decisions with competing lenses
best_used_when: The real risk is in blind spots rather than missing raw ideation.
You want structured disagreement before committing to a direction.
A wedge, roadmap, pricing, or go/no-go call needs more than one perspective.
typical_prompts: Run a product council on this.
Help me choose the wedge.
Do a roadmap council.

name: Spec Bundle
tagline: Build an implementation-ready bundle when a PRD is not enough.
focus: Execution-ready spec artifacts
best_used_when: Execution needs contracts, schema, tests, or architecture artifacts.
The build crosses subsystems or has important invariants.
A plain PRD would leave too much room for ambiguity.
typical_prompts: Turn this PRD into an implementation-ready spec bundle.
Add contracts, schema, and test plan.
Give me the minimum bundle I can hand to an agent.
origin_note: Inspired by Sereja Ris's PRD-first and draft-before-artifact planning patterns documented in sereja.tech. Canonical public source is spec-bundle-skill; agent-spec-bundle remains a synced mirror.

name: JustDoIt
tagline: Turn messy repo work into durable plan, status, and test-plan files.
focus: Durable execution planning for non-trivial repo work
best_used_when: A repo task is too big for a one-shot answer but too underspecified for immediate coding.
You want another Codex run to resume from repo files instead of reconstructing context from chat.
You need a clear first execution slice and explicit stop conditions before implementation begins.
typical_prompts: Turn this task into a ready-to-execute plan.
Write plan, status, and test-plan files for this repo task.
Prepare the execution docs before we start coding.
origin_note: Adapted from Sereja Ris's public justdoit skill, then rewritten into the current harness and routing setup.

name: Execution Pack
tagline: Build a durable Codex operating pack from a PRD or raw brief.
focus: Codex execution system with plan, status, tests, backlog, and prompts
best_used_when: Codex needs AGENTS.md, plan, status, tests, backlog, and reusable prompts instead of a loose spec.
The work will span sessions and should resume from repo state rather than chat memory.
You need explicit validation gates, blocker compression, and review-repair prompts for a serious build.
typical_prompts: Turn this PRD into a Codex execution pack.
Give me AGENTS, PLAN, STATUS, TEST_PLAN, and BACKLOG for this project.
Build a resumable execution pack with prompts for execute, resume, and blocker compression.

name: Triage Finding
tagline: Fact-check an external finding, detect duplicates, and give one honest verdict.
focus: Fact-checking usefulness triage for links, repos, posts, videos, and saved findings
best_used_when: You found a repo, article, post, screenshot, or video and need to know whether it matters now.
You want the claims checked against the primary source before acting on them.
A tool or skill may already exist locally and you want to avoid duplicate adoption.
A finding may deserve a skill, issue, note, or no action at all.
typical_prompts: Triage this.
Look what I found.
Is this useful for us?
Review this ideas folder.
origin_note: Codex-focused rewrite of the upstream Claude skill from alenazaharovaux/share.

name: Tool Scout
tagline: Search current tools across multiple sources and recommend the best-fit option.
focus: Multi-source build-vs-buy and ecosystem option research
best_used_when: You need current tool options before spending engineering time or vendor budget.
The answer depends on what exists right now, not on memory.
You want GitHub, MCP, awesome-list, and web signals instead of a one-source answer.
You want a short list of strong candidates instead of a giant comparison dump.
typical_prompts: Find tools for this.
What should we use for X?
Is there an MCP for this?
Find a Python library for PDF generation.
origin_note: Codex-focused rewrite of the upstream Claude skill from alenazaharovaux/share.

name: ADR Log
tagline: Record architecture decisions and trade-offs before reasoning disappears.
focus: Durable architecture and execution decision capture
best_used_when: A stack, schema, workflow, or vendor choice will be expensive to rediscover later.
You want trade-offs recorded in repo docs, issue state, or both.
A decision should stay visible even if it is later superseded.
typical_prompts: Record this decision.
Write an ADR.
Log why we chose this.
origin_note: Adapted for Codex from the adr skill in alenazaharovaux/share.

name: Issue Control Loop
tagline: Keep one GitHub Issue canonical for humans and agents.
focus: Canonical issue-driven workflow control
best_used_when: Work needs durable issue state and explicit handoff points.
You want machine-readable control state instead of vague chat status.
GitHub Issue and Project should become the real operator surface.
typical_prompts: Turn this GitHub issue into a clean control loop.
Use the issue as the canonical handoff surface.
Parse this PRD and make it issue-ready.
origin_note: Inspired by Sereja Ris's GitHub-as-memory and conversation-as-artifact workflow patterns, especially the context-separation model shown in ai-corp.sereja.tech.

name: GitHub Mobile Ops
tagline: Map Issues, Projects, and GitHub Mobile into a phone-first operator workflow.
focus: Phone-first GitHub operator workflow
best_used_when: You want to control work from a phone without losing canonical task state.
Issues, Projects, and GitHub Mobile are being used inconsistently and need a cleaner split.
You need to know what still requires a laptop and what can stay mobile-first.
typical_prompts: How should GitHub work from my phone?
Give me a phone-first issue and project workflow.
What belongs in GitHub Mobile versus desktop?

name: Continuity Ledger
tagline: Keep substantial work coherent with a factual CONTINUITY.md.
focus: Durable continuity across long-running work
best_used_when: The task will span sessions, branches, or long tool loops.
Chat memory is not a safe source of truth.
You need one short durable bridge between source artifacts and current state.
typical_prompts: Keep continuity on this task.
Update the ledger before we continue.
Do not let this drift across sessions.

name: Adversarial Review
tagline: Run a skeptical second-pass review that hunts for failures, not style nits.
focus: Findings-first second-pass review for risky diffs
best_used_when: A meaningful diff needs a real sign-off pass before anyone trusts it.
Agent-generated output looks plausible, but you want failure-hunting rather than approval theater.
The most important question is what can break, not how elegant the patch looks.
typical_prompts: Do an adversarial review on this diff.
Look for bugs and regressions here.
Give me a findings-first second pass.

name: Visual Explainer
tagline: Turn structure-heavy material into a clean self-contained HTML artifact.
focus: Browser-readable HTML explainers for systems, tables, and timelines
best_used_when: A diagram, system map, comparison, or wide table is clearer in a browser than in chat.
You want a shareable HTML artifact rather than a prose summary.
The main challenge is making structure visually legible.
typical_prompts: Visualize this.
Make a diagram for this system.
Render this table as HTML.
origin_note: Adapted for Codex from alenazaharovaux/share; that upstream skill credits nicobailon/visual-explainer as the original MIT base.

name: AI Writing Detox
tagline: Remove obvious AI-writing tics without flattening the real voice.
focus: Cleanup pass for public-facing drafts
best_used_when: A draft is directionally fine but sounds generically AI-written.
Public notes, posts, README copy, or build diaries need more trust and less stylistic fog.
You want a quick editing pass before publishing or committing docs.
typical_prompts: Make this sound less AI.
Detox this draft.
Clean up this README copy.
origin_note: Codex-focused rewrite of the upstream Claude skill from alenazaharovaux/share.

name: Public Artifact Lane
tagline: Turn real work into public-ready artifacts with evidence-first framing.
focus: Public-facing artifacts from real work
best_used_when: You finished real work and want a public artifact instead of leaving it in chat.
You need release notes, launch posts, or case studies grounded in actual changes.
You want a shareable writeup that does not invent results.
typical_prompts: Write a build diary from this work.
Make release notes.
Prepare a launch writeup.

name: Illustration Prompt
tagline: Turn vague image ideas and refs into precise generator-ready prompts.
focus: Visual direction and prompt structuring for image generation
best_used_when: You need a prompt for a hero image, card visual, cover, or campaign illustration.
Reference images should shape the result, but the user has not translated them into a precise brief.
The visual request is still too vague for reliable image generation.
typical_prompts: Write an illustration prompt.
Help me make an image prompt from these refs.
Turn this vague idea into a Midjourney prompt.
origin_note: Codex-focused rewrite of the upstream Claude skill from alenazaharovaux/share.

name: README Generator
tagline: Create or rewrite a human-first README without doc bloat.
focus: README quality and public repo framing
best_used_when: A repo needs a clearer public front page with a real quick start.
The current README is vague, stale, or reads like internal notes.
The project mixes multiple roles and needs explicit top-level taxonomy.
typical_prompts: Write a README for this repo.
Rewrite this README to be clearer for outsiders.
Refresh the quick start and usage sections.

name: Session to Post
tagline: Turn a finished coding session into a durable draft.
focus: End-of-session writing from real artifacts
best_used_when: A meaningful coding session is done and you want a build diary or post seed.
The story lives across diff, notes, and transcript and should not be rewritten manually.
You want end-of-session capture to be callable in plain language instead of only by CLI.
typical_prompts: Turn this session into a post draft.
Write a build diary from this diff and notes.
Make an end-of-session writeup from today's work.
origin_note: Inspired by serejaris/blog-pipeline-template and the dev-diary writing patterns published in serejaris/sereja.tech.

name: Video Builder
tagline: Assemble narrated videos from scripts with ffmpeg and TTS.
focus: Narrated video assembly with ffmpeg and edge-tts
best_used_when: You need to turn a script into a narrated mp4 quickly.
You want line-level visual timing without manual video editing.
You need a reproducible pipeline from text and stills.
typical_prompts: Build a short narrated video from this script.
Render a 2-minute explainer with edge-tts voiceover.
Assemble an mp4 from these lines and images.
